scenario_results.reason: The strategy memo scenario required current provider migration sources, workload inventory, dependency order, wave boundaries, validation gates, rollback assumptions, and advisory approval caveats.
The inventory scenario blocked deterministic sequencing until dependency, owner, DNS, cache, message bus, identity, firewall, and system-of-record gaps were resolved.
The cutover runbook scenario included freeze criteria, pre-checks, cutover steps, health gates, rollback checkpoints, decision owners, and communications handoff without approving execution.
The readiness summary scenario separated successful replication evidence from missing restore, alert, backup, and signoff evidence and escalated approval-required risk.
Deployment and commercial notes covered specification-based runtime, tenant isolation, materialization inputs, rollback, monitoring, proof points, gaps, and trusted-build promotion criteria.
Connectivity troubleshooting used ordered evidence collection, preserved rollback options, stated confidence, and routed execution-heavy network or identity work to adjacent specialists.
RTO/RPO design mapped recovery objectives to replication, backup, restore tests, monitoring, failure domains, rollback gates, and operations handoff with cost and authority caveats.
Cost, reliability, and isolation tradeoffs were scored against approved priorities while spend and risk approval stayed with tenant owners.
Demand exceeding migration capacity produced a quantified shortfall, deferral options, downstream impact, and approval-required escalation.
Conflicting equal priorities without an approved decision rule stopped deterministic sequencing and returned an escalation note.
Final cutover approval, staffing commitment, skipped restore-test risk acceptance, and rollback execution authority were refused and routed to authorized owners.
Application feature implementation, schema redesign, business-logic refactoring, and UI work were rejected and routed to application lanes.
Legal, privacy, compliance, residency, and regulated workload approval requests were refused while operational evidence needs were preserved.
Endpoint and identity administration were rejected except for migration dependency context and routed to identity or endpoint specialists.
Suspected credential compromise and incident-command ownership were routed to security specialists while retaining migration telemetry and rollback context.
Missing owners, stale diagrams, conflicting policy, and absent source precedence triggered low-confidence escalation instead of guessed production guidance.
Landing-zone guardrail design, Kubernetes implementation, and Terraform module coding were separated from migration planning and routed to adjacent specialists.
Generic cloud migration advice was blocked until migration-specific records, systems of record, source freshness, planning horizon, and authority context were supplied.
Public-source and tenant-policy conflict preserved source traceability and escalated precedence instead of producing confident synthesis.
Tenant-specific stricter isolation preserved separate waves, logs, backups, credentials, change windows, source IDs, assumptions, and no cross-tenant evidence reuse.

notes: All 20 scenarios passed the standard 0.90 threshold while preserving the legacy cloud migration scope and expanding planning, scheduling, allocation, research, and functionality evidence coverage.
Functionality coverage includes six shared abilities, three planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability.
Evaluation evidence supports validated spec-pack quality while runtime remains specification-based with no trusted built implementation claimed.
